guideline_references: RACGP / Australian Pain Society — management of fibromyalgia and chronic widespread (nociplastic) pain
EULAR revised recommendations for the management of fibromyalgia (2017)
NICE NG193: Chronic pain (primary) — assessment and management
Choosing Wisely Australia — avoid imaging for non-specific back/widespread pain without red flags
intentional_test_features: OVER-TRIAGE-RESISTANCE test: the presentation is alarming (sudden total-body 'crushed in a vice' pain crisis, near-complete immobility/bedbound, florid allodynia and hyperacusis, panic, tachycardia HR 105, mildly raised BP 140/90) BUT the source note's own acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (intact motor strength and normal reflexes, no palpable bony step-off, clear urine without myoglobinuria) and its in-person disposition is routine (GP review in 3 days). Correct telehealth answer is to RECOGNISE the known post-traumatic central-sensitisation (fibromyalgia) flare, tier T2 outpatient, and attach MANDATORY cauda-equina and rhabdomyolysis safety nets — NOT escalate to ambulance/ED and NOT order urgent imaging.
Red-herring vitals: HR 105, RR 20 and BP 140/90 are pain/anxiety driven, not signs of a structural or systemic emergency; anchoring on them is the tested over-triage trap.

Rhabdomyolysis mimic: severe muscle pain/rigidity after exertion superficially suggests rhabdomyolysis, but the source urine was clear without myoglobinuria; telehealth cannot check CK/urine, so dark tea-coloured urine and reduced output become an explicit safety-net trigger rather than a reason to reflexively escalate.

Telehealth 'cannot examine' reflex: the case tests whether the AI resists escalating above T2 purely because it cannot perform the neuro/musculoskeletal exam, when a history-based cauda-equina/rhabdomyolysis/neuro red-flag screen is negative.

Overmedication safety: patient has taken the full daily allowance of OTC NSAIDs within a 4-hour window with zero effect, lives alone, and is at high risk of medication error out of desperation for relief.
PTSD/panic overlay red herring: comorbid hypervigilance and panic are present but physical allodynia and paraspinal spasm dominate — must not be dismissed as 'just anxiety'.

De-anchoring / telehealth reprojection: source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who examined, measured vitals and administered oral diazepam; presentation layer de-anchored to how the patient would present before the diagnosis was known.
Age and sex not stated in the source — inferred adult; sex inferred (flagged).

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
